content:
Ryan, when a man swaps “the windows of heaven were opened” for “meteorological condensation event,” he hasn’t just changed vocabulary; he’s changed the kind of world he thinks he lives in.

Here’s what that shift tends to do over time:
- It thins reality. Scripture’s phrase names a personal God acting with purpose; the reduction names only impersonal processes. He moves from providence to mechanism. He may still believe in God on paper, but he stops seeing His hand in the world (Psalm 104; Job 38; Matthew 5:45).
- It starves worship. Poetry is the Church’s native language because it fits a world saturated with God. Trade it for lab-speak and you train your soul to admire processes rather than praise the Lord who sends rain in season (James 5:17–18).
- It breeds distance from Scripture. Instead of receiving the text on its own terms, he quietly places himself over it as its interpreter, flattening images into concepts. The Bible becomes data to manage rather than a voice to obey.
- It erodes moral meaning. In Genesis 7, “windows of heaven” is not only about water; it’s God’s judgment and the unravelling of creation’s order. Reduce it to weather, and judgment, mercy, and covenant recede into background noise.
- It withers the imagination. The same inner eye needed for parables, psalms, sacraments, and providence atrophies. Prayer becomes harder; gratitude grows thin; awe gives way to analysis.
- It isolates him from the communion of saints. He stops hearing Scripture with the Church’s ear—where “the heavens are telling the glory of God”—and ends up with a private, smaller universe.

What does it cost? Wonder, doxology, and the habit of seeing God everywhere. Call “I love you” a “neurochemical cascade” and you haven’t said something false, but you’ve missed the truth that matters most. So with rain: meteorology can describe the means; only “the windows of heaven were opened” names the Giver and His purpose.

Inerrancy doesn’t demand we replace the Bible’s metaphors with modern mechanisms. It asks us to take the text as God gave it—true in what it affirms, in the way it speaks. The better way is both-and:
- Affirm secondary causes without losing the first Cause. Say, “A cold front moved through—and the Lord sent us rain.”
- Let Scripture’s language retrain your perception. Pray the Psalms. Thank God out loud when it rains (Malachi 3:10). Read weather and history as theater for His faithfulness.
- Ask, each time you’re tempted to translate the Bible into antiseptic terms, “What truth about God’s character and action is this image revealing?” Then keep the image.

Over time that posture does the opposite work: it fattens the soul. You begin to live in a world where the Father opens windows, Christ upholds all things (Colossians 1:17), and the Spirit fills the earth. And that is a much truer world.
